coredata.dc:title: Ultrasound Shear Wave Propagation Modeling in General Tissue–Like Viscoelastic Materials
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of World Federation for Ultrasound in Medicine & Biology.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of World Federation for Ultrasound in Medicine & Biology.
coredata.dc:creator: Osika, Mariusz
Kijanka, Piotr
coredata.dc:description:
Objective This study aims to present an approach for the simulation of ultrasound elastic waves propagation in a diverse range of heterogeneous tissue-like viscoelastic materials, including, but not limited to, Kelvin-Voigt, Zener, Maxwell, Burger's, and Maxwell-Wiechert models, while also allowing for modeling highly viscous fluids.
Methods Ultrasound shear wave elastography (SWE) serves as a cost-effective modality for noninvasive, quantitative assessment of soft tissue viscoelastic mechanical properties. To explore tissue viscoelasticity, measuring the shear wave phase velocity in the frequency domain is a common method. In this paper, we employ modeling and numerical simulations to enhance the development of SWE methods. The study employs the staggered grid finite difference (SGFD) method along with recursive calculations of convolution integrals pertinent to linear viscoelastic models.
Results The presented numerical method demonstrates its capability to simulate the propagation of ultrasound elastic waves, both longitudinal and shear, across a broad spectrum of tissue-like viscoelastic heterogeneous materials. The approach successfully accommodates various viscoelastic models without requiring additional modifications in the numerical model, thus enabling a comprehensive exploration of different viscoelastic behaviors commonly observed in diverse tissue types.
Conclusion The developed combination of the SGFD method and recursive calculation of convolution integrals presents a novel and versatile approach in modeling linear viscoelastic tissue-like materials for SWE applications. This method eliminates the need for model-specific adaptations in numerical simulations, thereby offering flexibility for exploring and understanding diverse viscoelastic behaviors inherent in different heterogeneous tissue types, contributing significantly to the advancement of ultrasound SWE for diagnostic purposes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Finite difference
Viscoelastic
Kelvin–voigt
Maxwell
Zener
Burger's
Shear wave elastography
Elastodynamic
Acoustic
Soft material
Phantom

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Two-dimensional Staggered Grid Finite Difference (2D SGFD) Scheme
Analytical phase velocity calculation for viscoelastic materials
Heterogeneous case with single circular inclusions
Heterogeneous case with three irregular inclusions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of a model characterizing the shear behavior of a general viscoelastic medium.
Schematic representations of viscoelastic models characterizing the shear behavior of materials implemented in the SGFD simulation approach.

Normalized (a), (c) spatio–temporal shear wave velocity motion data and (b), (d) corresponding k–space spectra. The results are presented for the numerical SGFD tissue–mimicking homogeneous viscoelastic models.
Phase velocity dispersion curves. Results are presented for the numerical 2D SGFD tissue–mimicking homogeneous viscoelastic models along with analytical calculations.
Analytical phase velocity dispersion curves of materials used in heterogeneous numerical simulations with viscoelastic inclusions.
Normalized spatio–temporal shear wave velocity motion data and corresponding wavefields for selected values of time.

Time history plots of shear wave particle velocity profiles measured at three spatial locations, (a) before the inclusion, (b) at the center of the inclusion, and (c) after the inclusion. Results are shown for 2D SGFD numerical simulations for three different viscoelastic inclusion models.
Normalized shear wave velocity motion data wavefields for selected values of time.
Viscoelastic parameters used for homogeneous numerical models
Material parameters used in simulations for heterogeneous materials

head.ce:title: Ultrasound Shear Wave Propagation Modeling in General Tissue–Like Viscoelastic Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Robotics and Mechatronics, AGH University of Krakow, Krakow, Poland
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Robotics and Mechatronics, AGH University of Krakow, Krakow, Poland

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Robotics and Mechatronics, AGH University of Krakow, Krakow, Poland.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aims to present an approach for the simulation of ultrasound elastic waves propagation in a diverse range of heterogeneous tissue-like viscoelastic materials, including, but not limited to, Kelvin-Voigt, Zener, Maxwell, Burger's, and Maxwell-Wiechert models, while also allowing for modeling highly viscous fluids.

Ultrasound shear wave elastography (SWE) serves as a cost-effective modality for noninvasive, quantitative assessment of soft tissue viscoelastic mechanical properties. To explore tissue viscoelasticity, measuring the shear wave phase velocity in the frequency domain is a common method. In this paper, we employ modeling and numerical simulations to enhance the development of SWE methods. The study employs the staggered grid finite difference (SGFD) method along with recursive calculations of convolution integrals pertinent to linear viscoelastic models.

The presented numerical method demonstrates its capability to simulate the propagation of ultrasound elastic waves, both longitudinal and shear, across a broad spectrum of tissue-like viscoelastic heterogeneous materials. The approach successfully accommodates various viscoelastic models without requiring additional modifications in the numerical model, thus enabling a comprehensive exploration of different viscoelastic behaviors commonly observed in diverse tissue types.

The developed combination of the SGFD method and recursive calculation of convolution integrals presents a novel and versatile approach in modeling linear viscoelastic tissue-like materials for SWE applications. This method eliminates the need for model-specific adaptations in numerical simulations, thereby offering flexibility for exploring and understanding diverse viscoelastic behaviors inherent in different heterogeneous tissue types, contributing significantly to the advancement of ultrasound SWE for diagnostic purposes.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultrasound-based shear wave elastography (SWE) methods have been developed over the past two decades to measure various mechanical properties in many different organs [1]. SWE is an imaging modality that utilizes acoustic radiation force (ARF) to generate shear waves in soft tissues and ultrasound pulse-echo methods to measure the motion of the propagating waves [2]. The velocity of the waves changes as a result of the variation of the mechanical properties of the tissue, in particular, the elastic shear modulus and the shear viscosity.

SWE is utilized in various clinical applications, such as staging of liver fibrosis and characterization of tumors in the breast, thyroid, liver, and prostate [3–5]. In addition, SWE is also used for evaluating carotid atherosclerotic plaques, chronic kidney disease, and the heart [6–8]. Some clinical studies over the past few years have revealed SWE bias and variation across different manufacturer systems [9–12]. It is important to identify the source of these biases so that improved protocols can be defined to reduce their effects or at the very least make clinicians aware that certain practices may be advised. To understand some of the sources of variation, it is useful to have a validated simulation platform which can be used for shear wave propagation modeling in complex, viscoelastic media.

In most SWE applications, the medium is assumed to be elastic, homogeneous, isotropic, linear, and infinite. For this scenario, time-of-flight methods are typically used to estimate the shear wave speed [13]. For a viscoelastic medium, a dispersion effect is present, observed by wave velocity variations with frequency. Those variations can cause differences in the group velocity values obtained using time-domain methods. As a result, various techniques have been proposed which take advantage of the dispersion phenomenon by measuring the shear wave phase velocity at different frequencies [14–23]. In addition to viscoelasticity, the geometry of a medium can also cause dispersion. Waves propagating in materials that are thin with respect to the wavelength can undergo multiple reflections resulting in complex wave behavior with multiple modes and variation of phase velocity with frequency [24]. There are also situations where tissues have finite thickness and are viscoelastic, such as arteries, myocardium, bladder wall, and tendons [25–28].

Shear waves are complex in nature due to their dispersion and dynamic transient phenomena influenced by ultrasound frequency, focal depth and configuration, and transducer type (linear versus curvilinear) [29]. Other challenges are linked to bias related displacement estimation from ultrasound speckle signals and shear wave velocity estimation algorithms [30–32]. For example, mechanical noise resulting from natural motion such as breathing or pulsation, leads to displacement error and increased variance. Spatial averaging in the point spread function (PSF) when the moving area is smaller than the tracking PSF can also introduce bias in the estimated displacement. In addition, loss of speckle correlation resulting from the displacement of the speckles by the shear wave, leads to increased variance in the estimated displacement [32]. Hence, SWE is often supported by modeling and numerical simulations to enhance its diagnostic value. In particular, numerical techniques are used to understand how these different acquisition and material property parameters may affect the measurements of shear wave velocity. Modeling of shear waves generated by ARF excitations has been studied using the finite difference method (FDM) in transversely isotropic, viscoelastic, incompressible tissue–like materials [33], and the finite element method (FEM) in isotropic viscoelastic media [34]. Shear wave velocity results in a frequency domain investigated as phase velocity based SWE methods provide detailed information about viscoelastic media. It has been shown that different tissues exhibit varying viscoelastic behaviors, which can change when different frequency ranges are considered.

The proposed combination of the staggered grid finite difference (SGFD) modelling technique and recursion-based approach for simulating various viscoelastic tissue–like materials is the main novelty of this work. What sets it apart is its capability to model any type of viscoelastic material, homogeneous and heterogeneous with multiple inclusions of any shape, without requiring modifications in the numerical model. This fully general viscoelastic model not only provides flexibility in material selection but also opens up new possibilities for research and analysis. The ability to simulate wave propagation in a wide range of viscoelastic materials, including but not limited to Kelvin–Voigt, Zener, Maxwell, Burger's, and Maxwell–Wiechert models, allows for a comprehensive exploration of complex materials composed of multiple and variable viscoelastic components. The development of such a versatile tool addresses the limitations of existing numerical solutions, which are often restricted to a single viscoelastic model. This novel approach broadens the scope of investigations and facilitates a deeper understanding of viscoelastic behavior in various applications, including SWE.

The rest of this paper is organized as follows. First, the analytical formulas used for fully general viscoelastic media were described using the SGFD scheme. Then the validity of homogeneous and heterogeneous numerical models was tested and compared with the analytical calculations. Shear wave phase velocity dispersion curves were presented. The results are followed by a discussion and conclusions.

The elastodynamic stress equation of motion, namely the Navier- Lamé equation, in three-dimensional (3D) space is given by
(1)

∂ z

where
σ
i j
and
u i
represent the components of the stress tensor and displacement vector, respectively, for the material particles. The ρ parameter represents the density of the material. The subscripts i and j represent the Cartesian coordinates {x, y, z}. The propagation of elastic waves in 3D solid media is governed by a system of hyperbolic partial differential equations, as expressed in Eq. (1), in conjunction with the geometric definition of strains and the relationship between the stress and strain tensor components. In this work, the Cauchy strain tensor was utilized as a measure of the strain field.

This study focuses on the investigation of linear isotropic viscoelastic materials. This allows for the introduction of two relaxation functions, namely
G s
( t )
and
G p
( t )
, that fully characterize the material's behavior. It is important to note that these relaxation functions are zero for negative arguments. Using the
G s
( t )
and
G p
( t )
functions, the relationship between the

where, the subscripts s and p represent shear and pressure (longitudinal) waves, respectively. The aforementioned relationship is a convolution integral. The upper limit of the integration is determined by the fact that the relaxation functions are zero for negative arguments. On the other hand, the lower limit of the integration is generally assumed, which can be adapted according to the specific problem being investigated.

Equations (2 and 3) provide a straightforward physical interpretation of the relaxation functions. The shear relaxation function
G s
( t )
corresponds to the time-dependent changes of deviatoric stress that arise in the material due to its deviatoric strains, being a Heaviside step function. In other words, it is the step response of the deviatoric stress to the deviatoric strain. Similarly, the bulk relaxation function
G p
( t )
can be interpreted as the step response of the volumetric stress to volumetric strain.

Using definitions of the volumetric and deviatoric components of the stress and strain tensors, one can express the fundamental hereditary integral formulation for a linear isotropic viscoelastic solid as follows
(4)

δ

)

d τ .

Both relaxation functions,
G s
( t )
and
G p
( t )
,
can be defined independently to characterize a general viscoelastic medium. Such a medium can be effectively modeled as a combination of parallel connected elements: a pure elastic element, a pure viscous element, and a finite number of Maxwell elements.

Figure 1 shows a schematic representation of the model that characterizes the shear behavior of the aforementioned medium. The parameters µ and η represent the elastic and viscous components, respectively, that are connected in parallel and correspond to the Kelvin–Voigt model. The model can be simplified to the well–known Maxwell–Wiechert model when the parameter associated with the viscous element η is set to zero. For a more general viscoelastic model, multiple parallel–connected Maxwell elements can be incorporated. Each of these elements is characterized by the parameters µr and ηr (where r = 0...N), which represent the elastic and viscous components of the respective element. The relaxation time for each Maxwell element can be determined as
T r
( s )
=
η r
μ r
.

Likewise, a general viscoelastic model governing the relationship between volumetric stress and strain,
G p
( t )
,
can be introduced and characterized by K and ζ, representing the elastic and viscous components, respectively. Furthermore, the parameters Kr and ζr characterize each of the M parallel–connected Maxwell elements, where a relaxation time can be determined for each element as
T r
( p )
=
ζ r
K r
.

The shear and bulk relaxation functions corresponding to general viscoelastic models can be written in the form
(5)
G s
( t )
= μ + η δ
( t )

∑
r = 1

e

∑
r = 1

e
−
t
T r
( p )
,
where
δ ( t )
is Dirac's delta function [35]. Terms with a sum sign are Prony series characterizing parallel-connected Maxwell elements. The relaxation functions for Maxwell elements are obtained from order differential equations
(7)

k k
∂ t
.

From the mathematical structure of the Maxwell model, it can be seen that it allows modeling of highly viscous fluids [36]. The characteristics of this type of material correspond to a fluid at low frequencies and to an elastic body at high frequencies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Two-dimensional Staggered Grid Finite Difference (2D SGFD) Scheme
ce:section.ce:para:
The theory described in the previous section was adapted to the SGFD numerical method. To reduce computational costs, a two-dimensional (2D) numerical model was considered. In the SGFD scheme the velocity–stress formulation of the elastodynamic equation was used. Partial differential equations composed of Eqs. (1) and (4) were transformed into the following first–order hyperbolic system
(9)
ρ
∂
v x
∂ t
=
∂
σ
x x
∂ x
+
∂
σ
x z
∂ z
+
F x
(
x , z , t
)
,
(10)
ρ
∂
v z
∂ t
=
∂
σ
x z
∂ x
+
∂
σ
z z
∂ z

F z
(
x , z , t
)
,
where

=
(
K +
4 3

)

+
(

)

∂ z

)

(14)

T r
( · )

v i
( 0 )
∂ j
where the subscripts i and j denote the spatial dimensions x and z, respectively, and the superscript (·) refers to pressure (p) and shear (s) waves.

In the numerical analyses considered in this work, the material was assumed to be in an unstressed state prior to the designated time zero. As a result, the lower limit of the integration in Eq. (14) was adjusted to reflect this assumption.

In Eqs. (11–13) additional terms can be observed, which are associated with the time differentiation of Eq. (4). These terms are necessary to accurately account for the time evolution of the stress tensor [37]. The parameters
υ x
and
υ z
represent the components of the velocity vector of the medium particles. The x and z coordinates have been intentionally omitted to shorten the notation. The specific focus of this work is on 2D wave propagation. As a result, equations related to the direction y were omitted, assuming invariance along this direction. The x–axis was assumed to be horizontal while the z–axis was vertical.

The iterative equations for the 2D SGFD method were derived using a scheme provided by Virieux [38]. The spatial derivatives were discretized using a centered finite scheme. Numerical velocity vector components
υ x
,
υ z
and numerical stress tensors
σ
x x
,
σ
z z
,
σ
x z
were computed based on an explicit scheme.

The introduction of Maxwell's elements to the material definition requires the evaluation of 4M + 6N convolution integrals at each step. Direct calculation of these integrals based on the convolution integral definition can be time consuming and memory intensive. This is because, for the current step, it is necessary to know the velocities at each node of the model for each previous time step. To overcome this problem, a recursion formula was employed for each integral in Eqs. (11–13). The recursion formula allows each new solution to be calculated by updating the previous solution, eliminating the need to store and access the velocities at each time step for the convolution integrals. By utilizing the recursion formula, the computational burden associated with the convolution integrals is significantly reduced. This approach improves the efficiency of the calculations and avoids the memory and time limitations associated with direct calculations of the convolution integrals based on the integral definition.

Instead of analyzing each integral separately, a general approach has been considered that can be applied to all of them. The recursive technique for evaluating the integrals is as follows [39,40]:
(15)
q
( t )
=
∫ 0 t
e
−
t − τ
T
∂
v ~
( τ )
∂ τ
d τ +
e
−
t T
v ~
( 0 )
.

The value of the function q at each time step,
t n
, can be calculated using the value of this function at the previous time step
t
n − 1
and the value of the time derivative of an arbitrary function
υ ~
( t )
(16)
q n
=
e
−
Δ
t
T
q
n − 1
+
Δ
q n
,
where
(17)
Δ
q n
=
∫
t
n − 1
t n

−
t n
− τ
T
∂

( τ )
∂ τ
d τ ≅

n
−

Δ

∫

−
t n
− τ
T
d τ .

In Eq. (17), the time derivative was assumed to be constant between time steps
t
n − 1
and
t n
, and the time derivative was evaluated using a finite difference. The integral can now be calculated analytically directly over each time step as
(18)
Δ
q n
=
v ~
n
−
v ~
n − 1
Δ
t
T
(
1 −
e
−
Δ

T
)
.

The assumption of q 0 =
υ ~
( 0 )
allows the second term in Eq. (15) to be included in the calculations. This assumption was made to ensure consistency in the calculations and to facilitate the recursion process. This provides a stable recursion for both small- and large-time steps, and produces very smooth transitions under variable time steps [40].

In order to mitigate undesired reflections from model boundaries, various absorbing boundary conditions are typically employed. The complex-frequency shifted perfectly matched layer (PML) based on recursive integration was applied in this work [41]. The formulation is derived independently from the grid definition, allowing for its application on any grid. One advantage of this PML formulation is its ability to improve data quality when dealing with grazing angles [41]. The approach was adopted for the SGFD scheme.

In this study, the SGFD method was used to model the mechanical wave propagation phenomenon in viscoelastic tissue-like materials. The dimensions of the simulated domain were x = 0–40 mm in the lateral direction and z = 0–40 mm in the axial direction. The modeled region was surrounded by a 40 mm wide PML area. The entire model was discretized into sufficiently small square cells. The grid dimensions were set to Δx = Δz = 0.1 mm. The time step for the simulations was evaluated based on the Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy (CFL) condition [42]. To maintain the numerical stability the CFL value was set to 0.5.

In order to excite propagating shear waves in bulk materials, a simplified ARF push beam was applied in a form
(19)
P
(
x , z
)
=
P 0
e
−
(
(
x −
x 0
)
2
2
d x 2
+
(
z −
z 0
)
2
2
d z 2
)
,
where, P 0 is the magnitude of the body force, and
P
(
x , z
)
=
F z
(
x , z
)
. The parameters dx and dz specify the spatial distribution in the lateral and axial directions, respectively. The dx = 0.2 mm and dz = 2 mm were used. The x 0 and z 0 values represent coordinates of the focused ARF push location, and were set to 20 mm in both directions. The ARF push beam was excited for 50 µs.

The resulting velocity wavefield was sampled at 10 kHz and then used for further data processing. The numerical procedure was implemented in MATLAB R2022a release (MathWorks, Natick, MA, USA) software using the parallel computation technology, offered by modern Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) and Compute Unified Device Architecture (CUDA) utilized in low–cost graphical cards. The computations were conducted on a computer featuring an Intel i7-11700K @ 3.6 GHz processor, an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 graphics card, and 48 GB of RAM.

In the numerical models, a constant density, ρ , of 1000 kg/m3 was assumed. The study focused on the analysis of shear wave propagation in soft tissues, hence for each numerical model the bulk relaxation function was defined as Gp (t) = K. This function resulted in the elastic behavior of the bulk strain
ε
k k
and stress
σ
k k
. To ensure that the longitudinal wave propagated much faster than the shear wave, a value of the bulk modulus K = 40 MPa was selected. This choice allowed the longitudinal wave to quickly reach the PML region where it was attenuated, while the wavefield associated with the shear wave remained undisturbed. The resulting longitudinal wave velocity was approximately
c p
( p )
≅
200 m/s.

By varying the number of parameters, different viscoelastic models were simulated, allowing for a comprehensive analysis of shear wave propagation in different viscoelastic materials. Two sets of parameters were considered for each model, denoted as Models 1 and 2. The numerical results obtained from these simulations were then compared with analytical calculations to validate the accuracy of the implemented algorithms.

In the second scenario, the focus was on modeling shear wave propagation in heterogeneous materials. An elastic background material with viscoelastic circular inclusions was considered. Inclusions with a diameter of 10 mm were located at the geometric center of the modeled region. This set of numerical models aimed to demonstrate the capability of the proposed SGFD scheme for simulating various types of inclusions, such as different stages of cancerous tissue growth. Three numerical models were prepared, each incorporating a different viscoelastic inclusion: Kelvin–Voigt, Maxwell, and Zener models. The parameters for these models, as well as the background tissue, were summarized in Table 2 .

The third scenario focused on examining a heterogeneous phantom containing multiple irregular inclusions. Each inclusion exhibited different viscoelastic material parameters, i.e., Kelvin–Voigt, Maxwell, and Zener models. The same viscoelastic models and material parameters were used as in the second scenario, i.e., the Kelvin– Voigt, Maxwell, and Zener models.

There are different methods that have been used to calculate the phase velocity dispersion curves from measured data, including nonparametric, parametric and high–resolution methods [20,21]. In many cases, the two–dimensional Fourier transform (2D-FT) of the spatio-temporal particle motion
υ (
x , t
)
is used to generate the “k–space” represented by the wavenumber, k, and frequency, f. For the ARF excitation often used in SWE applications, the k–space representation will have a distribution of energy covering a bandwidth that is related to the push beam geometry, push temporal length, and the mechanical properties of the medium. The peaks of the k–space distribution are related to the phase velocities of different wave propagation modes. These peaks can be found by searching in orthogonal directions either along the f or k directions where the f direction was used in this work. In this manuscript, a comparison between dispersion curves calculated for the SGFD and analytical dispersion curves was utilized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical phase velocity calculation for viscoelastic materials
ce:section.ce:para:
In the case of a linear body, steady–state time harmonics of angular frequency ω are accompanied by time harmonics of stress of the same angular frequency. Upon substitution of deviatoric strains
ϵ
i j
=
ϵ
i j
*
e
− i ω t
and volumetric strains
ε
k k
=
ε
k k
*
e
− i ω t
into Eqs. (2–3), and evaluating the time integrals, the following relationship results
(20)
s
i j
= 2
G s *
( ω )
ϵ
i j
*
e
− i ω t
,
(21)
σ
k k
= 3
G p *
( ω )
ε
k k
*
e
− i ω t
,
where
ϵ
i j
*
and
ε
k k
*
are the complex time–independent amplitudes of deviatoric and volumetric strains, respectively. It follows from this notation that a viscoelastic body in the frequency domain can be characterized by the
G s *
( ω )
dynamic shear modulus and the
G p *
( ω )
dynamic bulk modulus. Furthermore, the stresses can be written in the form
s
i j
=
s
i j
*
e
− i ω t
and
σ
k k
=
σ
k k
*
e
− i ω t
, where
s
i j
*
and
σ
k k
*
are the complex amplitudes. From Eq. (4) and the adopted deviatoric and volumetric forms of strains and stresses, there is a relationship between the complex amplitudes of the components of the stress and strain tensors
(22)
σ
i j
*
=
δ
i j
(
G p *
( ω )
−
2 3
G s *
( ω )
)
ε
k k
*
+ 2
G s *
( ω )
ε
i j
*
.
This implies that the stresses and strains are out of phase and energy is dissipated during the steady–state oscillations of material particles. Furthermore, the attenuation depends on the frequency.

In this study, the viscoelastic solid is modeled as a combination of elastic, viscous and a finite number of Maxwell elements connected in parallel. The dynamic modules of each element can be summed to obtain the dynamic modules of the entire model. Therefore, the
G s *
( ω )
and
G p *
( ω )
modules can be expressed as
(23)
G s *
= μ + i η ω +
∑
r = 1
N
ω
η r
ω
T r
( s )
− i
,
(24)
G p *
= K + i ζ ω +
∑
r = 1
M
ω
ζ r
ω
T r
( p )
− i
.

The analysis of Eqs. (1) and (22) indicates that in a linear isotropic viscoelastic medium, two shear waves are polarized in the horizontal or vertical direction, and one longitudinal wave can propagate [43]. For shear waves, the following dispersion relation can be written
(25)
ξ
2
=
ρ
G s *
( ω )
ω
2
,
where, ξ is a wavenumber. The relationship that describes the dispersion of longitudinal waves is given by
(26)
ξ
2
=
ρ
G p *
( ω )
+
4 3
G s *
( ω )
ω
2
.

In Eqs. (25) and (26) the wavenumber ξ is complex, indicating that the amplitude of propagating time harmonic waves decreases with increasing distance of propagation. This phenomenon is commonly referred to as wave attenuation in viscoelastic media. The imaginary part of the complex wavenumber represents the attenuation coefficient, which quantifies the rate at which the wave energy is attenuated as it propagates through the medium. As the propagation distance increases, the imaginary part of the wavenumber leads to a decay in the amplitude of the waves, resulting in their gradual weakening.
Based on Eqs. (25–26) complex phase velocities for shear and longitudinal waves can be defined as

(27)
c c
( s )
=
G s *
( ω )
ρ
,
(28)
c c
( p )
=
G p *
( ω )
+
4 3
G s *
( ω )
ρ
,
and the real parts of the phase velocities are calculated as follows
(29)
c p
( s )
=
(
Re
(
1
c c
( s )
)
)
− 1
,
(30)
c p
( p )
=
(
Re
(
1
c c
( p )
)
)
− 1
.

The relationship presented in Eq. (29) was used to determine analytical shear wave phase velocity values, which were then compared with the numerical results obtained from the implemented viscoelastic models. This comparison served as a validation of the numerical simulations, ensuring that the implemented models accurately captured the behavior of propagating shear waves in various viscoelastic materials.

This paper considers elastic waves propagating in an almost incompressible body in which the phase velocity of the longitudinal wave is much greater than the phase velocity of the shear wave
c p
( p )
≫
c p
( s )
. This means that the relaxation function
G p
( t )
, and hence the dynamic modulus
G p *
( ω )
, can be assumed to be constant and equal to the bulk modulus K. Nevertheless, longitudinal waves are dispersive and also damped due to the presence of the
G s *
( ω )
term in Eq. (26). However, if the bulk modulus K and the real and imaginary parts of
G s *
( ω )
satisfy the inequalities
K ≫ Re (
G s *
( ω )
)
and
K ≫ Im (
G s *
( ω )
) ,
it can be shown that the phase velocity corresponding to a longitudinal wave is approximately real and independent of frequency. Consequently, the bulk wave can be treated as a non–dispersive wave with a phase velocity equal to
c p
( p )
≅
K ρ
.

In this section, the results for the 2D SGFD calculations are presented. First, the results corresponding to the homogeneous models are shown. Phase velocity curves calculated for each numerical model are compared with the analytical phase velocity values. Then, the results for heterogeneous models with single circular inclusions and multiple inclusions of random shapes are presented.

Figure 3 presents spatio–temporal shear wave particle velocity motion data and corresponding k–space spectra. The particle velocity motion data were measured at the focal depth. Results were presented for numerical models with various viscoelastic parameters summarized in Table 1. An elastic and five different viscoelastic models, each with two sets of parameters, were shown.

For the elastic materials, the amplitude of the shear wave decreased as it propagated due to geometric spreading and the nature of the excitation. However, in the case of Kelvin–Voigt materials, the wave attenuation became more pronounced. The presence of a viscous component in the material led to a faster decrease in the wave amplitude as it propagated. Additionally, it is important to note that the width of the spectrum increased with frequency, which is associated with higher shear wave attenuation at higher frequencies. This phenomenon can be observed in the spreading of the spectrum in k–space (Fig. 3b, 3d second row).

In the case of the Maxwell material, the rapid attenuation of the wave was observed, which was expected for this type of viscoelastic material. The results for the Burgers model showed similar behavior to the Maxwell model due to its similar structure. However, the Burgers model incorporates two additional parameters, allowing for a more comprehensive characterization of the material over a wider range of frequencies. While the phase velocity in the Maxwell model monotonically approaches a finite value as the frequency increases, the introduction of an additional element in the Burgers model can lead to a different monotonic increase in the phase velocity. This improved behavior of the Burgers model allows for a more accurate description of the material's response over a wider frequency range. In addition, these viscoelastic models, including the Maxwell and Burgers models, are well–suited for modeling high–viscosity fluids.

For the Zener material, the shear wave was observed to propagate over a certain distance, which was also attenuated. This behavior is attributed to the presence of an elastic element in the structure of the Zener model. A comparable phenomenon can be observed for the material described by the Maxwell–Wiechert model. In both cases, the spectrum of the shear wave was less spread, indicating lower wave attenuation compared to the previous models. By appropriately selecting the model parameters, it is possible to characterize viscoelastic materials within a specific frequency range. In addition, models that have a larger number of parameters offer the advantage of more accurately characterizing the response of viscoelastic bodies over a wider frequency range.

Figure 4 shows shear wave phase velocity dispersion curves. The results were presented for the numerical SGFD tissue–mimicking homogeneous viscoelastic phantoms and analytical calculations. A very good convergence between the numerical and analytical results was observed. The agreement between the analytical and numerical results confirmed the accuracy of the numerical scheme used.

It was observed that the phase velocity values for models that include an elastic element in their structure are non–zero at zero frequency, while for models without an elastic element, the phase velocity starts from zero. This behavior was expected due to the presence or absence of an elastic component in the material model. Additionally, it was noted that, except for the Kelvin–Voigt model, the phase velocity values stabilized or became bounded. This indicates that the addition of a viscous element to the model structure leads to a monotonically increasing phase velocity as the frequency increases. Although this behavior may seem nonphysical, it allows for an effective representation of the material properties within a specific frequency bandwidth.

The investigated models demonstrated good performance and accurately reproduced the analytical phase velocity values within the considered frequency range. The results obtained clearly indicate the successful implementation of the proposed numerical procedure, which combines the SGFD method with the recursive evaluation of convolution integrals. Moreover, this study successfully demonstrates a novel application of the SGFD method to model any isotropic linear viscoelastic material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heterogeneous case with single circular inclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In this numerical scenario, viscoelastic inclusions within an elastic background were considered. The tested models for the isotropic material inclusions contained the Kelvin–Voigt, Maxwell, and Zener models. The material parameters for each model are given in Table 2. In addition, Figure 5 shows the frequency dependence of the shear wave phase velocity for each viscoelastic model, as well as for the elastic background tissue–mimicking material.
Figure 6 presents the normalized spatio–temporal shear wave velocity motion data

(Fig. 6a) and wavefields at selected time points, t 1 = 5.1 ms, t 2 = 9.1 ms, and t 3 = 13.1 ms (Fig. 6b, 6c, and 6d, respectively). The dashed black lines indicate the geometric region with inclusions locations. For the inclusion described by the Kelvin–Voigt model, the shear wave demonstrated a higher propagation velocity compared to the background material, in line with theoretical expectations. The amplitude of the generated wave, however, has decreased due to the local viscosity present in the inclusion.

For the Maxwell model, the wave in the inclusion region had lower velocity compared to the background material, and it was also more attenuated than in the Kelvin–Voigt model. These observations were consistent with the characteristics of the Maxwell model and its ability to introduce damping effects.

In the case of the Zener model, the wave velocity in the inclusion region remained almost unchanged compared to the background material, as expected based on the selected parameters. However, local damping was present, and the amplitude of the shear wave was larger compared to the Maxwell model and comparable to the Kelvin–Voigt model. These results demonstrate the behavior of the Zener model in capturing the viscoelastic properties of the material.

In all three cases, the presence of a reflected wave from the inclusion was evident. The amplitudes of the reflected waves were comparable for the Kelvin–Voigt and Zener models, indicating similar behavior in terms of wave reflection. The Maxwell model, on the other hand, showed the highest amplitude of the reflected wave, suggesting a stronger reflection from the inclusion.

Similarly, shear wave amplitudes behind the inclusion can be analyzed. The highest amplitude was observed in the case of a model with the Kelvin–Voigt type inclusion, indicating a significant wave transmission and limited energy dissipation. The Zener type inclusion followed with a slightly lower amplitude, suggesting a moderate level of wave transmission and damping. The lowest amplitude was observed in the case of the Maxwell type inclusion, indicating a higher degree of wave attenuation and energy dissipation. These observations highlight the differences in shear wave behavior and interaction with different viscoelastic inclusion models, providing insights into their respective properties and characteristics.

The time histories for three measurement points, with an axial coordinate of z = 20 mm and the lateral coordinates of x = 10 mm, x = 20 mm, and x = 30 mm, were shown in Figure 7 . These time history plots provide insights into wave amplitude variations at different spatial locations within the model. By analyzing the time history plots at the specific measurement points, one can observe the variations in wave amplitudes over time and assess the differences between the models. This analysis helps to understand how the wave propagates and interacts with the inclusion and the surrounding elastic background at different spatial locations within the simulated domain.

At the first measurement point (lateral coordinate x = 10 mm, Fig. 7a), the initial pulse corresponds to a shear wave propagating in the elastic medium, and its amplitude is identical for all models due to the use of the same background material for each case. However, the amplitude of the reflected wave differs among the models, with the Maxwell–type inclusion exhibiting the highest amplitude. For the Kelvin–Voigt and Zener models, the amplitudes of the reflected waves were comparable, but the Kelvin–Voigt model showed a slightly higher amplitude.

For the measurement point at the center of the inclusion (Fig. 7b), the time domain waveforms demonstrate significant attenuation of the excited wave in the Maxwell model inclusion. In contrast, the results for the Kelvin–Voigt and Zener models are similar, although the wave was more attenuated in the Zener model. Additionally, it is evident that the wave propagated with the highest velocity in the Kelvin–Voigt model, followed by the Zener and Maxwell models, within the inclusion region.

At the measurement point with a lateral coordinate of 30 mm (Fig. 7c), the wave propagation speed in the inclusion region and the level of attenuation can be clearly observed. The Kelvin–Voigt model exhibited the fastest wave propagation within the inclusion, followed by the Zener and Maxwell models. In terms of attenuation, the Maxwell model showed the most significant damping, followed by the Zener model, and the Kelvin–Voigt model exhibited the least amount of attenuation.

Overall, these observations provide insights into the behavior of propagating shear waves in different models and highlight the variations in shear wave propagation velocity and attenuation among the Kelvin–Voigt, Zener, and Maxwell models.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heterogeneous case with three irregular inclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, a numerical model with three irregular viscoelastic inclusions was investigated to demonstrate the feasibility of modeling heterogeneous phantoms containing many irregular inclusions covering a limited area. The inclusions were characterized by three different viscoelastic materials: Kelvin–Voigt, Maxwell, and Zener, marked with black solid, dotted, and dash–dotted lines, respectively. In this case, the material parameters corresponding to the elastic background and individual inclusions were taken from the previous numerical case described in Section 3.2, for which the data are summarized in Table 2. A simplified ARF push beam was used to excite propagating shear waves, but in this case a longer push in axial direction with dz = 4 mm was used.

Figure 8 presents the results at eight selected time points for the considered numerical model. As expected, the shear wave velocity increased in the Kelvin–Voigt inclusion, and the wave also experienced partial attenuation. Additionally, reflections resulting from the parameter changes between the inclusion and the background were observed. Notably, both primary and side reflected waves were noticeable due to the irregular shape of the inclusion. Subsequently, the propagating wavefront encountered a Maxwell-type inclusion, where it underwent further attenuation. Significant reflections arising from the interaction of the excited wave with this inclusion were also visible. It is worth noting that the irregular geometry led to the presence of multiple reflected waves. Next, the wavefront encountered a Zener-type inclusion with parameters closely resembling the elastic background in the low-frequency range. As anticipated, a slight increase in shear wave velocity and partial attenuation were observed. For this inclusion, reflected waves with smaller amplitudes compared to the previous cases were also visible.

These results demonstrate the potential of the proposed numerical model to study the interaction and behavior of various heterogeneous materials, which are composed of numerous viscoelastic inclusions with varying shapes.

This paper presents a unique, general solution for modeling any chosen linear isotropic viscoelastic material using the SGFD simulation approach based on recursive calculation of convolution integrals. A general theoretical model of a linear isotropic viscoelastic material was presented and an algorithm for its implementation was described. The focus of this work was on modeling of shear waves propagating in nearly incompressible tissue–like materials.

The proposed scheme was tested and validated through a series of numerical simulations. The simulation results were compared with analytical values to assess the accuracy of the proposed method. Spatio–temporal data and corresponding k–space representations were generated to analyze the shear wave propagation characteristics. By examining the real part of the phase velocity of shear wave propagation in different viscoelastic materials, the accuracy of the proposed technique was assessed. The obtained results show that the proposed method accurately reflects the analytical characteristics.

It is evident that the most attenuated waves occur in materials that lack an elastic element in their structure. These models are suitable for modeling highly viscous fluids. For these materials, the value of the dynamic modulus, and therefore the phase velocity, is zero at zero frequency, unlike other materials that contain an elastic element. For materials with a viscoelastic element, its disadvantage becomes apparent as there is an infinite increase in stiffness and a corresponding increase in phase velocity. This can lead to discrepancies between numerical models and laboratory measurements for a wide range of excitation frequencies or with a high center frequency. On the other hand, materials without this element show a monotonically increasing dynamic modulus and phase velocity with increasing frequency, but tending towards a limited value. Therefore, the presented approach offers significant possibilities for tissue modeling and characterization over a wide range of frequencies, providing a better understanding of shear wave propagation in soft tissues.

The developed numerical approach provides a wide range of modeling possibilities for various types of soft tissues with different characteristics. It can be used to characterize tissue viscoelastic properties and create databases for different tissues within relevant organs, along with standard parameter values. This enables the examination of patients and comparison of their results with predetermined values, aiding in early diagnosis and assessment. In addition, the tool facilitates the simulation of various types of load conditions, allowing the investigation of optimal approaches to studying specific materials described by selected viscoelastic materials. Such versatility and flexibility contribute to a deeper understanding and analysis of tissue behavior, providing valuable insights for medical research and clinical applications.

Using the proposed numerical tool, the viscoelastic inclusions were also developed and simulations were performed to test the feasibility of modeling shear wave propagation and scattering on inclusions characterized by different viscoelastic materials. The obtained results align with the anticipated behavior, confirming the effectiveness of the presented technique in modeling heterogeneous materials. This expands opportunities for further research, as most existing work primarily focused on the Kelvin–Voigt material due to its ease of implementation.

It is worth noting that this approach allows to use of many different viscoelastic materials within one heterogeneous model. By incorporating different viscoelastic materials, it becomes feasible to develop new numerical models for tissue inclusions, including cancerous tissue at various stages of growth. This opens up avenues for using simulation techniques in the advancement of SWE early detection methods. Furthermore, the proposed approach provides an opportunity to assess and enhance existing methods for detecting tissue pathological changes.

In this work, a simplified ARF push beam was used to generate propagating shear waves. However, the proposed method remains unchanged when utilizing realistic ARF pushing beam geometry, which can be generated using e.g. Field II or FOCUS simulation tools [44,45]. The SGFD method can be used for wave propagation in any heterogeneous material of arbitrary shape and complexity. The method discretizes the structure under investigation into a grid of cells. The material properties are assumed to be constant within each cell but may vary between cells. If the cells are taken to be sufficiently small, this assumption represents a good approximation to realistic complex media. The 2D ARF push beam can be assigned directly to a grid of cells, similar to the process carried out with the simplified ARF push described in Eq. (20).

The computational time for every 2D simulation performed in this study did not surpass 15 minutes. It will vary based on the medium being considered and the parameters of the numerical model, including grid size, time step, and the chosen material model.

Material parameters of viscoelastic models should be carefully selected to reflect the specific features of the modeled tissue. This may include experimental data, empirical relationships, or theoretical calculations based on tissue properties. Similarly, numerical parameters such as grid size and sampling time should be chosen appropriately to ensure model stability and accuracy. The grid size should be fine enough to capture the relevant spatial details and variations in the tissue structure, while avoiding excessive computational costs. The sampling time should be small enough to accurately capture the temporal behavior of the waves, particularly the high–frequency components. The choice of these parameters should be guided by a combination of theoretical understanding, experimental data, and validation studies. Sensitivity analyses can also be conducted to investigate the influence of parameter variations on the simulation results.

One of the limitations of this study is that 2D isotropic numerical models were investigated. The presented approach can be extended to simulate shear wave propagation in more complex anisotropic and 3D cases, offering opportunities to study and understand wave behavior in a wider range of tissue structures and configurations.

This paper presents a solution for numerical modeling of linear isotropic viscoelastic materials using the combination of the SGFD scheme, the recursive calculation of convolution integrals and the PML implementation. The main focus of this study is on modeling shear wave propagation in tissue-like materials. The proposed method provides various modeling capabilities for soft tissues. It eliminates the need for any additional efforts or modifications in the numerical model to accommodate different viscoelastic behaviors. Modeling of multiple viscoelastic inclusions using different viscoelastic materials demonstrates the applicability of this technique for modeling heterogeneous tissues. This approach can be extended to a 3D case and can also be used to simulate wave propagation in linear viscoelastic and anisotropic soft tissues. The results contribute to a better understanding of shear wave propagation and offer significant opportunities for tissue characterization and modeling.
The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Center in Poland under research project no. UMO–2021/43/D/ST8/01295.
